data.title: Franklin Core Plus Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Market sentiment improved across the third quarter of 2025 (Q3) due to a partial resolution of some of the outstanding trade policy concerns. At its September meeting, the US Federal Reserve (Fed) cut the fed funds rate by 25 basis points (bps) and signaled that more cuts were likely to come by the end of this year. This bolstered markets which viewed that the cut was addressing some of the weakening economic measures, such as the status of the US labor market. As per Bloomberg, fixed income spread markets performed well as corporate bond spreads reached multidecade lows by the end of the quarter. Other fixed income sectors, such as securitized, also posted positive returns. The benchmark 10-year US Treasury yield fell eight bps to end the quarter at 4.15%.
  • Contributors: Overweight investment-grade (IG) corporate bonds and agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS).
  • Detractors: Security selection in senior secured-floating rate bank loans and HY and IG corporate bonds.
  • Outlook: There is high uncertainty about future Fed policy, even between members, as reflected in a large distribution seen in the Fed’s scattered projections for the path of fed funds for the remainder or the year. In our view, this level of uncertainty has the potential to spur volatility in the UST market.

Performance Review

  • Sector allocations were a strong driver of overall performance for the quarter as spreads moved tighter across most sectors, leading to excess returns. Our out-of-index exposure to a number of sectors—including HY corporate bonds, floating rate bank loans, collateralized loan obligations (CLOs), residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS), and agency collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs)—helped returns for the period. Additionally, overweight allocations to IG corporate bonds, MBS, and emerging market debt contributed to results.
  • Security selection weighed on performance, led by selection in floating-rate bank loans and HY and IG corporate bonds.
  • Yield curve positioning hurt results primarily due to an underweight exposure to the 10-year area of the yield curve.

Outlook

  • In our view, the Fed is unlikely to cut interest rates soon. Despite recent signs of cooling inflation, Fed officials remain cautious due to ongoing tariff-related uncertainties. Companies are expected to raise prices as pre-tariff inventories diminish, leading to a likely rise in core goods inflation. Furthermore, average US tariff rates remain historically high despite a temporary easing in US-China trade tensions. We therefore expect potentially higher inflation and slower growth. With inflation still above target and risks skewed to the upside, we expect the Fed to maintain its pause. Amid still-resilient industrial production and retail sales, we believe the Fed is likely to stay on hold until there is clear evidence of labor market deterioration without a simultaneous rise in inflation.
  • The “One Big Beautiful Bill” spending package will further increase the US deficit over the next decade. Combined with factors such as uncertain trade policy (which has raised stagflationary concerns) and an expansionary fiscal policy (which implies greater debt issuance), we expect longer-term rates to continue to climb higher through the rest of the year.
  • For the corporate credit sectors, we maintain our emphasis on diligent security selection and individual company fundamentals. IG credit fundamentals have remained supportive. While we believe IG corporate bonds continue to offer relatively attractive yield, spread levels offer little cushion for further economic, market or geopolitical surprises. In our view, spreads are likely to remain in a trading range but, at current levels, are more likely to widen than tighten. In the lower-credit quality HY and floating-rate bank loan sectors, although we remain broadly constructive amid generally supportive technical conditions, we continue to favor quality companies amid persistent market bifurcation and idiosyncratic opportunities as we evaluate each company’s fundamentals and exposure to different possible policy alternatives.
  • For securitized sectors, our generally neutral stance remains largely unchanged. Prepayment risk remains minimal, and mortgage credit quality remains high. The fundamental and technical outlook for the sector remains favorable, with attractive relative value to IG corporates. We also anticipate increased demand from banks if the yield curve normalizes or regulatory uncertainty dissipates. For RMBS, heightened macroeconomic uncertainty due to the Trump administration’s fiscal and foreign policies and tense geopolitical climate could result in more periods of elevated rate volatility and “risk-off” episodes. We therefore prefer a defensive positioning at the top of capital stack in higher-carry-profile bonds with limited extension risk. Despite our generally cautious stance, we are finding value and identifying opportunities in higher-quality fixed income investments.

data.title: Armada Hoffler Properties: The Valuation Disconnect Persists
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered Armada Hoffler Properties (AHH), I highlighted their focus on deleveraging and shifting towards more predictable rental income while still paying a solid and affordable dividend even after a cut.

With the stock nearly flat since then despite a solid earnings report and mixed signals from the macro environment, they are still trading at what I believe to be clearly undervalued levels, which is why I’m reiterating their Buy rating.

Internal Developments

AHH reported a solid Q3, beating both the market’s revenue and FFO estimates by quite a bit, while the AFFO reached $19.075 million during the quarter and ~$57.84 million during the first 9 months of 2025. Annualized, that’s roughly $77.12 million per year, which is certainly not bad for a ~$718.5 million market cap, placing them at a P/AFFO ratio of about 9.3.

However, note that that’s still a significant drop on a per share basis ($0.57 in 9M’25 vs $0.76 in 9M’24) as the number of shares increased significantly to fund the completion of their Southern Post mixed-use development and deleverage the balance sheet. The dilution doesn’t have to be bad in the long-term, while the company shifted from relying on volatile third-party construction fees to focus on more stable rental income.

AHH also narrowed their 2025 Normalized FFO outlook to $1.03 to $1.07 per share, helped by higher NOI that helped offset their construction adjustments, while also giving some guidance regarding Southern Post stabilization.

As a note, the overall weighted average (stabilized) occupancy rate was 95.7% back in Q3 2025, which is still solid in this market. Per branch, that’s 96% for retail, 96.5% for office and 94.2% for multifamily.

Financially, based on AHH’s latest report, we see an overall normal position for a REIT, with limited cash but significant assets that more than offset their debt.

We can see that their debt increased compared to 2024 especially following the July $115 million senior notes (inaugural private placement with maturities of 3, 5 and 7 years and a blended interest rate of 5.86%), but the management expects this ratio to drop naturally in 2026 as the EBITDA would increase thanks to property stabilization and they potentially sell non-core assets to pay down the debt. As the company’s CFO mentioned during the Q3 Earnings Call:

Capital markets remain selective, and we are structuring our balance sheet to reflect that reality. With our debt private placement completed in July and our liquidity stabilized through prudent cash management, we have the ability to remain patient and disciplined as the cycle evolves.

AHH currently trades at a strong dividend yield of ~8.14%, which is above their preferred (AHH.PR.A) that currently pays ~7.89%. The preferred stock is callable from June 2024, but it really wouldn’t make much sense to call them now when buybacks would theoretically achieve much more, as these are 6.75% yielding preferred shares based on their cost basis for the capital originally raised ($25 par value), so the risk-reward on them is very solid.

As they say, the AFFO payout ratio stands at 74.9%, and after taking out the non-cash interest income, we should look at about 93.9%. They also mentioned the following regarding buybacks:

I don't want to say we're going to get into buyback land, but certainly, there's an attractive accretive opportunity there. I'm not sure that we'll take that versus long-term property -- kind of income-producing property. But yes, that's what we are doing right now, especially given the price of the equity and how that's trading in today's market.

Macro Developments

Although rate cuts advanced at the end of 2025, we continued to see significant uncertainty in the macro environment, with pressure both from international factors (e.g. Venezuela, Greenland, Iran) and also domestic ones (e.g. Fed independence, consumer weakness, real estate policies, etc.), which can ultimately result in an even bigger delay between the Fed’s actions and the actual cost of external capital for the companies and people.

Ultimately, being a REIT limits the company’s ability to fund new initiatives (especially during a strategic shift) as they have to pay most of what they make back to us, relying on external capital (issuing stock, issuing debt, asset recycling) for funds. In this environment, it’s no wonder that REITs are underperforming, as on top of occupancy/tenant issues, they also have to face significantly higher costs.

As we can see in AHH’s case, issuing stock is nearly out of the question since it would cost about 8% (the dividend - unless they cut it; would cost even more if they increase it in time) and they are actually considering the opposite (buybacks), while the most recent debt issuance came with a 5.86% weighted average interest, which makes the ~7% or so interest they can generally get on their investments really low for this cost.

This was a completely different story before the pandemic, when issuing stock cost about 4% to 5% and the SOFR was much lower.

Especially following a 9-3 vote in favor of the December cut (biggest disagreement since 2019) based on the December minutes as some participants see it fitting to pause now, combined with the DOJ criminal investigation into Powell and rising fear around the Fed’s independence after Powell’s term ends in May 2026, I believe there is risk of seeing capital costs at least remaining elevated for longer - if not even increase.

This would technically be the market’s way of saying it wants a better yield for the new political/economic/etc. risk it would take when lending to the US, potentially leading to more international diversification as the US could risk eroding its safe haven status.

However, looking at it from a longer-term perspective, if we see an improving consumer/business environment combined with the general fall in new supply across all of AHH’s real estate segments, there is certainly room for a recovery in both broader economics and the market’s opinion/demand for AHH and similar high-yield stocks.

This fall in new supply comes after a post-pandemic boom in many segments of real estate, while the market is now facing rising costs of building new properties (higher construction, workforce, debt, etc. costs) combined with weak occupancy and uncertainty, which can turn this around.

Risks Reiterated

Although a broader market recovery is possible, the company still faces high capital costs in an overall weak environment, with potential for the environment to get worse before any potential positive impact from the supply side would be visible, which could also affect their potential asset sales.

Especially as a big chunk of their long-term debt is due over the next couple of years, any near-term issue in the environment could have negative implications for years ahead, hurting the company even long after these issues are over.

Valuation

Starting with AHH’s annualized AFFO of $77.12 million in 2025, I’ll assume a 6% CAGR over the first 5 years thanks to their project pipeline, debt repayments and potential for lower interest expenses, with a 3.25% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, as well as an 10% discount rate and 102.09 million shares outstanding as of their Q3 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $1.18 billion and an intrinsic value of $11.55 per share, which hints at a very strong margin of safety compared to the current $6.88, being well above this level even if we use much more conservative estimates.

Conclusions

AHH remains a Buy, as the stock continues to be undervalued in an environment with strong potential to improve while the company is delivering solid results.

There is obviously risk as we’re talking about a REIT, which exposes them significantly to external capital costs, but the overall risk-reward is attractive from these levels, while the dividend yield and potential buybacks on top are also offering a very nice boost to the investment case.

data.title: The Great Rotation: Money Flows Out Of Big Tech And Into Small Caps
data.description:
If you got lost in what were largely meaningless headlines yesterday, you missed what was going on beneath the surface—rotation. Stocks opened lower after bank earnings failed to impress investors. We had delayed reports on retail sales and the Producer Price Index for November, which are so dated that they are not even worth commenting on. Hopes for a Supreme Court ruling on the legality of tariffs were dashed by late morning, with the next possible date being Wednesday. Of course, we had another barrage of political and geopolitical headlines, but few are relevant to what is now the most overarching investment theme in markets today.

As I asserted would happen at the beginning of this year, investors continue to rotate out of the expensive technology sector with a decelerating rate of earnings growth and into smaller companies that have more reasonable valuations and greater increases in earnings growth. While the S&P 500 has gained 1.2% so far this year, and the Nasdaq Composite is less than 1% higher, the Russell 2000 small-cap index has soared nearly 7%. The equally weighted S&P 500, which best represents the average stock, has gained 3.7%, while the Magnificent 7 (MAGS) are collectively down 1.4%. Stocks left behind during the first three years of this bull market are moving to the front of the pack.

There is nothing unusual about this, as stocks respond to rates of change. As I outlined in my 2026 Outlook, the rate of earnings growth for the Magnificent 7 decelerated last year to approximately 22% from more than 37% the year before. It increased from 5% to 9% for the remaining 493 names.

The rally in small caps started during the second half of last year, as investors anticipated a far more robust rate of change in earnings for the Russell 2000 index this year. It is as simple as that.

This strength also reflects confidence in more robust economic growth from monetary and fiscal stimulus, and we are starting to see sectors of the economy that have been stagnant come back to life. One that stands out is housing, which I wrote about last week as being a low-risk opportunity in advance of several efforts by the Trump administration to stimulate demand. Yesterday, we saw some of the first signs of life. Existing home sales rose in December at the fastest pace since 2023. Sales rose 5.1% over the previous month to an annualized rate of 4.35 million, which is the highest figure since February 2023. Additionally, the median sale price of an existing home sold rose just 0.4% from a year ago to $405,000. That is disinflation at work.

New home sales declined 0.1% in October over the prior month to an annualized rate of 737,000, but that is 18.7% above the year-ago level. We are finally above pre-pandemic levels for new home sales. The median price of a new home sold was $392k, which is down 8% from the prior year, while the average price was $498k, which is down 4.6% from the prior year. Some of this has to do with homebuilders offering incentives to move inventory, but that is not a bad thing for consumers.

Affordability is also improving as incomes are now rising at a faster rate than existing home prices, and the cost for a 30-year fixed-rate mortgage is gradually approaching 6%. The National Association of Realtors’ affordability index, which tracks the average family’s ability to qualify for a loan, is now at its highest point in nearly three years.

The housing market may not come roaring back, but I see gradual progress throughout 2026, which bodes well for a sector of the economy that has been stagnant for several years. I also see tremendous upside in companies benefiting from the recovery, especially those in the business of mortgage origination, servicing, and search, as well as retailers focused on home furnishings, appliances, renovation, and repair. There might be a few small caps in there too!

data.title: 2 REITs That Pay Me $1,000 Each Month
data.description:
One of the main reasons why I invest so heavily in real estate is that it provides high and consistent income.

With regular stocks, I feel like I'm completely at the mercy of the market, and I don't like it. It feels much more speculative as my returns are entirely dependent on whether other investors are willing to bid up the share price or not. It works well when the market is rising, like in recent years, but as we go through times of declining or stagnating prices, you may be left with nothing for years.

With real estate, I'm at least getting paid generously to wait. Each property has tenants, typically tied to a long-term lease, that pay you monthly to use the property. This helps a lot to stay patient as I earn a decent return regardless of what the market does next.

Today, we're going to look at two of my biggest monthly dividend-paying REITs (VNQ) that pay me a total of $1,000 each month.

Over time, I expect them to grow in value as well, but even if they don't, I'm more than happy to simply collect my growing passive monthly income.

BSR REIT (HOM.U:CA / BSRTF)

We start with the lowest yielder.

BSR REIT (HOM.U:CA / BSRTF) is today offering a 4.6% dividend yield paid on a monthly basis. That may not seem high, but keep in mind that the REIT retains about 40% of its cash flow to reinvest in growth, which has allowed it to grow its dividend at a rapid pace in recent years.

This REIT focuses on apartment communities in the rapidly growing Texas Triangle of Dallas, Austin, and Houston:

These markets are experiencing some of the fastest population and job growth in the entire nation:

Even then, BSR's average rent is only about $1,500 per month, which represents a low 20% rent-to-income ratio on average.

I think that as these markets keep growing at a rapid rate, they will continue to see their rents rise to higher levels over time.

But this, of course, isn't a perfectly steady process. In recent years, these markets became oversupplied as too many developers thought to capitalize on this opportunity and launched projects back when interest rates were ultra-low. This led to stagnating rents and occupancy rates in 2024 and 2025.

But now conditions are again improving. The new supply under construction has dropped to a decade-low level and continues to decline further each month that passes. Developers are finishing what they have underway, but very few new projects are breaking ground because it has become very difficult, if even possible, to build new communities at a discount to replacement cost.

Put simply, the high inflation in construction costs and the higher interest rates have made most new development projects economically unfeasible, and that's especially true at the rent level at which BSR is leasing its properties.

This means that we're likely to see little new supply over the next few years, even as these markets keep attracting a lot more people, which should ultimately lead to solid rent growth:

Despite that, BSR is today priced at an exceptionally low valuation. The recent oversupply, coupled with the surge in interest rates, has caused its growth to stagnate and hurt its market sentiment. As a result, it now trades at a 30% discount to its net asset value, which is truly exceptional for a REIT with such attractive long-term prospects.

The management is taking advantage of this low valuation to buy back shares hand over fist. This should create a lot of value for shareholders over time, and as we go into the apartment bull market, I expect the valuation discount to close, potentially resulting in 40% upside from here.

While we patiently wait for the upside, we earn a steady 4.6% dividend yield, paid monthly, and that still leaves significant cash flow for the REIT to keep reinvesting in growth.

That's far more attractive than what you could achieve on your own by buying a private apartment community, in my opinion: Higher yield, lower valuation, completely passive, diversified, and liquid.

Hard to beat that.

Canadian Net REIT (NET.UN:CA)

Now we move to a higher yielder.

Canadian Net REIT (NET.UN:CA) is my single largest monthly dividend-paying REIT investment.

It is currently offering a 6.5% dividend yield, paid monthly. Just like BSR, its dividend is very well covered with a low 55% payout ratio, which leaves significant cash flow for the REIT to keep reinvesting in growth.

So far this year, it has grown its cash flow by 9% and also hiked its dividend by 1.5%, and that's just the tip of the iceberg.

The REIT has one of the best growth track records of any REIT. Just look at how fast it has managed to grow its cash flow over the long run:

How has it achieved this?

I have previously described the REIT as an early-day Canadian version of Realty Income (O) in that it's much smaller, operates with more leverage, and faces less competition for acquisition, and this allowed it to earn higher spreads and grow at a faster pace.

It's the only REIT focusing on net lease properties in Canada, targeting primarily service-oriented properties such as grocery stores, quick-service restaurants, pharmacies, and gas stations, which it then leases on a long-term basis under triple-net leases.

They generate highly consistent rental income as their leases have many years left on them, and rents are pre-agreed for the entire duration of the lease, with automatic annual rent escalations. The tenants are also typically responsible for all property expenses, including even the maintenance of the property.

And the beauty of operating in Canada is that their interest rates are already a lot lower at just 2.25% at the short end of the curve, yet their cap rates are just as high, if not higher, than in the US because there's a relative lack of buyers.

This allows Canadian Net REIT to consistently earn superior spreads, which results in faster FFO per share growth, and given that it's still very small in size with its $300 million portfolio, we think that it should be able to keep this going for a long time to come.

Despite growing at a rapid pace, the REIT is priced at a low valuation of just 8x FFO and a 6.5% dividend yield that's safely covered and paid monthly.

I think that as interest rates continue to decline and yield-starved investors return to the REIT sector, this REIT will regain popularity and is likely to reprice at a materially higher valuation.

This is why I have heavily invested in this REIT, and it now generates more passive monthly income than any other monthly-paying stocks in our portfolio at High Yield Landlord.
