data.title: Blue Owl Capital: Price Reset Creates A Compelling Total Return Opportunity
data.description:
Blue Owl Capital (OWL) merges two strong theses that often attract income investors' attention: resilient private credit income combined with AI infrastructure-led demand. My thesis supports OWL's positioning as a decent way to earn yields while earning from long-term secular growth.

OWL's credit discipline holds good so far, and the data center exposure seems to reduce default risks. I also see better payout coverage ahead, driven by earnings power improvements. For a stock where price fluctuations are the primary driver of total returns, the current yields, when combined with the risk-reward asymmetry, make for a compelling total return bet. The business quality remains good, and the support from data centers should help a Buy rating. Speculative total return investors can consider a small position, anticipating execution to eventually rerate the stock even higher.

Income and Embedded Expectations

The Fee Related Earnings (FRE) reported in Q3 2025 were $0.24 per share. Distributable Earnings (DE) was $0.22 per share. The quarterly payout of $0.225 therefore translates to a payout ratio of ~102%. This is not a hugely underfunded payout but shows that OWL relies on future growth to a large extent to normalize the payout ratio over time and even add some conservative buffer, ideally. So, my expectations on dividend growth are subdued for the near term. The dividends are stretched now, and the best-case scenario is that they are maintained and better funded.

The share price is now trading at ~16x FRE (annualizing $0.24 FRE per share reported in Q3). This is definitely not distressed valuations. I collated quarterly FRE data since 2022-23 and used quarter-end share prices as an indication of FRE multiple-based valuations (indicative because OWL share prices fluctuate materially intra-quarter). The valuation trends show an expected compression from the rate-supported highs. They are comforting in the sense that they do seem to respect the rate normalization regime underway and do not seem to have been unusually blown up due to the AI narrative that has emerged in the past couple of years.

The FRE and DE growth trends over the past year also look supportive and explain why markets are still assigning healthy valuations in an otherwise mid-cycle macro environment. Management has also indicated meaningful acceleration in FRE and DE numbers through 2027. So, OWL's current payout ratio is definitely not looking threatened.

Another way to look at valuations is the yield itself. A close to 30% correction in share prices in the past year has mechanically boosted yields. This shows that share prices have indeed retracted to a level that reflects the macro conditions. These yields are now more meaningful on a cost basis, and as long as the more dominant total return driver—share prices—can prove a favorable risk-reward asymmetry, the yields become a supporting factor for the Buy thesis. I will pause here and remind readers that I look at OWL as a total return vehicle (given its price volatility)—not a pure income vehicle alone.

Optionality and Favorable Risk-Reward

There are some already identified earnings drivers that can be tapped into. This could support upside in share prices even without valuation rerating. Management highlighted ~$28b of AUM not yet paying fees and expects ~$360m in management fees from these assets.

I also see the AI infrastructure exposure as a stabilizing influence in OWL's portfolio, if not a margin growth driver. What is opening up now for OWL now is long-dated contracted revenues with investment-grade counterparties and the potential to add volume and scale, if not margins. The economics are also protected from early termination. As per management commentary in Q3 2025,

If there were an early termination, there is a perfectly mathematical make whole where we make -- the debt makes all its money.

Even if AI exposure may not immediately mean improved unit economics, it expands the fee-paying AUM over time, which supports FRE growth. This raises the floor on credit stress outcomes due to protected long-dated contracts with high-quality tenants.

Risks to the Thesis

The biggest risk to the Buy thesis for OWL is execution misses. The deployment of expected fees from AUMs not paying fees currently may reduce odds of FRE growth and share price upside, even if the floor may still be well defended at current valuations.

I will also watch payout ratios over the next 2-3 quarters to see if stretched payouts are still underway. Any dividend cut in itself may not be concerning for the Buy thesis, as it relies on total returns. However, income appeal could trigger further compression in valuation.

The usual credit deterioration risks due to stress in broader credit markets also exist but do not seem material today. Average annualized losses are still looking reasonable -

As we have highlighted in previous earnings calls and continue to call out, the health of our credit portfolio remains excellent with an average annual realized loss of just 13 basis points and no signs of meaningful stress. In direct lending, the modest level of nonaccruals we have seen are not thematic in nature, and there's not been an uptick in our watch list levels.

Good Entry Point

Finally, a look at historical 3-year total return regimes shows the 3-year rolling returns have never been negative, and the current 3-year total return of ~45% lies at the lower end of the spectrum. This reinforces the fact that this is not an overheated regime fresh entries today will walk into. A local low in 3-year returns also implies either a deteriorating business model, which is not the case looking at the AI contracts and growth in FRE. Therefore, this reset is more about valuations and sentiment while the earnings durability outlook remains intact.

A lower 3-year total return is not necessarily a timing tool that signals reasonable forward returns, but it increases the odds of favorable outcomes over the next 3 years. And supports the bull thesis based on fundamentally sound pillars like FRE growth, stable credit performance, and improving earnings visibility.

data.title: From K-Shaped To J-Shaped: A 10% Credit Card Cap Has A Winner
data.description:
Introduction

Things are moving really fast this year. We have almost forgotten about Venezuela, even though it happened less than two weeks ago. After all, almost every day we have seen breaking news that has shaken some sectors in the market, with Greenland, Iran, and Jerome Powell catching most of the attention.

However, a small parenthesis of the daily debate was filled by the news that President Trump wants a 10% cap on credit card rates for one year, a measure that could be effective as early as January 20. The reaction was immediate as credit card stocks as well as credit card issuers fell sharply.

To say it all, this proposal should not have been a surprise, since back in February 2025, Senator Sanders introduced the "10 Percent Credit Card Interest Rate Cap Act", which is currently being discussed. However, President Trump's words placed this bill at the center of the domestic political discussion, up to the point that several banks had to warn that this choice would limit access to credit.

Here, I would like to discuss a few aspects that I have not seen truly considered. First and foremost, in the case the bill is approved, we will have to see whether it will be applied only for one year, as the President said, or until January 2031, as it is written in the bill's text.

Since it is because of the President's statement that we are considering the cap rate, I will mainly focus on the consequences of a cap for one year.

The Problem

The 10% cap appears as a solution to a problem that "The Consumer Credit Card Market" report, just issued at the end of December 2025, clearly highlights: the cost of credit, measured through average percentage rate (APR) levels, is at decade-highs. The report highlights that while cardholders increased 9% from 2022 to 2024, the overall amount of interest charges increased by 52%, which created an extra $45B in charges driven by higher rates and not by strong growth in the cardholder base.

Now look at the chart below. The orange line shows the commercial bank interest rate on credit card plans at the end of 2025 (the report I mentioned above stops at the end of 2025), where we see that the current APR is 21.4% (and this is an average; there are credit cards that charge up to 30%). At the same time, the purple line shows that credit cards and revolving plans have been rapidly increasing after the pandemic. When we put these two lines together, we see that while rates were skyrocketing, credit card balances also exploded. It is something we would not think based on what we know about macroeconomics: when rates go up, usually the consumer borrows less. It happened just the opposite; the consumer is borrowing more. Surely, this is linked to another fact that I talked about recently: extra savings from the pandemic have long run out, and the savings rate is currently very low. In other words, we are seeing loan demand driven by necessity. In other words, credit card loans are a non-discretionary item, and they play the role of an income substitute or strengthener.

This is the issue. American households are short on savings. It is not so simple, though. As we have recently seen in another article I wrote, credit card debt by income group is quite different: it is the middle class that is borrowing more than anyone else, while lower-income households are starting to reduce their debt after it peaked in early 2024, and high-income earners are actually carrying less debt now than they did before the pandemic struck.

Considering that a little over two-thirds of the U.S. GDP comes from consumption expenditures (see chart below), the fact that APRs are high implies that the consumer, especially the middle class, is now particularly pressured and strained even though, as JPMorgan's CEO just said during the last earnings call, "consumers have money", pointing out that employment is high and that the OBBBA provides meaningful stimulus.

Now, we better understand one of the elements that drives rates so high. While the Fed is actually lowering rates, banks have to price a premium for the risk of delinquencies, which is something that can't be simply ruled out, given the average balance sheet of American households.

This is not the place to teach or preach. But, surely, this situation shows why a pillar of healthy household finance should be simply to avoid credit card debt and learn to live a bit below one's means. This would also have the effect of having access to nice perks such as cashback programs.

The Effects of the "Solution"

Now, there is no doubt that if the cap were to be approved for a long period of time, we would probably see a shock in credit supply: card approvals would rapidly decrease, private label cards could be flushed down the toilet, and weaker borrowers would probably lose access to the credit market. The middle class, in particular, would be exposed to loan sharks or start turning to pawn shops, which usually apply "service fees" that represent real APRs of up to 500% (now, that's usury!). Delinquencies would increase (the New York Fed calculated that, as of Q3 2025, around 4.5% of outstanding debt is in one of the delinquency stages).

Let's tackle the easy part of this paragraph: credit card issuers. If rates are capped at 10%, the negative delta is 11.4 percentage points. Below, you see how revolving consumer credit increased; as of November 2025, it stood at $1.31T. This means that the gross interest revenue reduction for credit card issuers could be close to $150B a year. This is what's at stake, and it is a huge sum. Of course issuers would tighten their issuances and would lower credit card limits to reduce the risk. A price ceiling, therefore, would put pressure on risk premia, pushing them down. This would squeeze weaker borrowers out of the credit market. I would bet new marketing campaigns would target higher-income households, offering them any sort of deal. A waterbed effect could also take place: while credit card rates move down because of the cap, credit card issuers could raise prices elsewhere to compensate (an annual fee for just holding a card could be a solution, for example). Here, the scenario is clear: lower earnings for credit card issuers and fewer consumers participating in the money lending and borrowing market.

However, I think that the market is getting it wrong with network companies, namely Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA). I don't think these companies are directly vulnerable because they actually don't care a lot about APR. The real consequence could be that, if credit tightens, volumes could become softer. But, in reality, when we deconstruct how the APR cap would work, we see that it is meant to transfer profits away from the issuers to benefit the borrowers.

Now, here is where a short-term scenario kicks in. I said above that I wanted to deal with the one-year cap. If this is what the President wants to be approved by Congress, then things could change compared to the doomsday narrative that we are hearing.

First of all, if people do believe that the cap will only last one year, then I don't think card issuance could be severely hurt. Issuers would perhaps be a bit more cautious, but they would also benefit from the fact that, after one year, they would be able to earn higher interest on a higher amount of debt. We have to be realistic: if the APR is lower, I don't see it likely for households to suddenly change their spending habits and become strict observers of Dave Ramsey's hate for debt. I actually see the contrary happening: it will feel cheaper to borrow money. Which means that credit card issuers are simply postponing their high-margin earnings to the next year. True, after a year there could be some sort of deal that caps rates at 15% or around that. A deal would have to be found eventually, but only if we consider a longer timeframe. A year goes by quite quickly.

This is the bridge argument that leads us to the consumer. Unfortunately, due to the last government shutdown, the latest economic release updates have been delayed, and we are still stuck with the data from last September, when personal income grew 0.4%. However, the Fed's last G.19 report on consumer credit shows that revolving credit decreased 1.9% YoY in November, which is awkward since, during the holiday season, these balances usually increase. It could be a sign that some households are pulling back on revolving credit because it has become truly too expensive.

If this is the correct interpretation, then it becomes easier to support the following argument: a one-year cap would probably fuel a spending increase due to the fact that borrowing suddenly becomes cheaper. A lower rate would also reduce the efforts of many consumers to pay down their debt, which would also contribute to an increase in overall credit card debt.

Technically, we could see a move from a K-shaped economy to a shallow J-curve, where we see a sudden improvement in spending, which would be followed by a slowdown as issuers adapt to the new cap by tightening underwriting and bringing down credit limits. But the second part of this curve could be avoided if the cap lasts just one year.

Do you see where this leads? What happens within one year? Midterm elections. I don't want to raise a political discussion, but I would like to highlight that this topic comes out in a year when how consumers feel about their finances and the overall economy matters more.

Finally, we should not underestimate what could happen inside the financial world during this year. I am in the field of those who believe that financial companies will be among the first beneficiaries of AI since they have huge datasets and can automate many internal tasks. One year with a cap would speed up the process toward AI that has already started, pushing banks and other credit card issuers to change their internal cost structure to be more competitive. As the year expires and rates can move higher, many of these companies could find themselves with a leaner cost structure, thus seeing that even a lower APR is sustainable and can lead to hefty margins. This might be too speculative, but I would not overlook the efficiency gains that many financial stocks will benefit from.

Conclusion

As a result of what we have discussed, I am not concerned by the 10% cap rate, and my overall moderately bullish look on the market is not changed. I have indeed bought more of Visa in recent days and will buy more of Mastercard in the next few, as I think investor fears around these two, especially around the latter, which has great cross-border exposure that would not be affected by the cap, are overblown.

data.title: TSMC: Set For New All-Time Highs
data.description:
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) aka "TSMC" submitted better-than-expected earnings for its fourth fiscal quarter amid strong demand for AI-optimized chips. TSMC achieved record profitability in Q4 '25 due to robust demand for GPUs and AI accelerators, resulting in the fab reporting its seventh consecutive quarter of 10%+ year-over-year earnings growth. Further, shares are very reasonably valued, based off of earnings, and the chip enterprise is likely going to grow its revenues another ~30% this year as demand for AI-driven accelerated computing is expected to remain strong.

TSMC continues to control the fab/chip manufacturing market based off of Trendforce data which makes the enterprise a strategic long term investment for investors that have a favorable view on the Data Center market and accelerating adoption of AI technologies.

Previous rating

I rated shares of TSMC a Strong Buy -- Strong Buy At All-Time Highs -- in October 2025 due to the fab being able to expand its top line as well as its earnings in light of strong secular demand for new AI chips. Demand for new semiconductors that are optimized for artificial intelligence applications has been surging, and TSMC indicated with its Q1 '26 outlook that it expects robust demand to continue.

TSMC also beat its own gross profit margin outlook for Q4 '25 and with semiconductor sales likely to continue to surge in 2026, the foundry play will remain a top growth investment for investors, in my opinion. I expect TSMC to achieve new all-time highs in 2026 and I also believe that the firm's earnings scorecard for Q4 '25 sets other chip plays like Nvidia (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) up for new all-time highs this year.

AI rally set to continue for TSMC in 2026

In the fourth fiscal quarter TSMC generated better-than-expected revenue and earnings due to a highly beneficial demand situation in the high-performance computing segment: the Taiwan-based chip giant generated $3.14 in normalized EPS, beating the consensus estimate by $0.16. In the terms of revenue, TSMC generated a top line of $33.7B which out-matched the consensus estimate by $1.0B.

TSMC achieved strong revenue growth in the fourth-quarter, due to the company serving the largest chip design companies, including Nvidia, AMD, and Intel (INTC) with chip manufacturing services. In FY 2025, the company generated a total T$3.8 trillion in revenue, showing 31.6% year-over-year growth... which the chip enterprise already revealed in last week's preliminary results. In the most recent quarter, Q4 '25, TSMC saw 25.5% year-over-year growth (in USD terms) and posted total revenue of $33.7B.

TSMC benefited from red-hot demand for its 3nm, 5nm and 7nm semiconductors that are used in Data Centers in order to power AI applications. The firm's outlook, discussed further below in the valuation section, indicates that the chip company expects this momentum to last, driven by the HPC segment.

In the fourth quarter, the chip maker generated a total gross margin of 62.3%, showing 3.3 PP growth compared to the year-earlier period. Further, the fab easily exceeded its own margin guidance which called for a gross profit margin of 59-61% in Q4 '25. In the previous quarter, TSMC's gross profit margin was 59.5%, so the company squeezed out a nice 2.8 PP margin gain here as well.

As a result of booming AI chip-related revenues and gross profit margin expansion, TSMC generated record profits of T$505.7 billion in Q4 '25 which beat the consensus estimate of T$478.0 billion by a wide margin. TSMC's net income increased 35% year-over-year, meaning the fab's profit growth is about 10 PP higher than its revenue growth in USD terms.

The outlook for 2026 is also very positive and investors will likely see somewhere around 30% top line growth on a full-year basis if TSMC can maintain its current growth momentum, which I believe is likely. Gartner’s projections for 2026 indicate that the Data Center market will continue to expand at double-digits, mainly due to growing CapEx spending on AI-capable chips and other server infrastructure.

With Data Center spending to edge higher in 2026, TSMC's high-performance computing segment is primed for out-performance. HPC continues to dominate TSMC's revenue mix, with the segment representing more than half (55%) of all revenues in the fourth-quarter. Importantly, since Gartner's projections imply double-digit growth (19% Y/Y) in the Data Center system market in 2026, there is a good chance that the demand situation will work in favor of TSMC.

TSMC’s valuation

TSMC has a number of strategic benefits that could translate into a higher earnings-based valuation multiplier in 2026. Most importantly, TSMC's outlook for Q1 '26 highlights strong top line growth expectations as well: the firm's guidance for the first-quarter of FY 2026 calls for revenues between $34.6B and $35.8B which implies a year-over-year growth rate of 38%.

What is most compelling about TSMC is that the fab has an uncontested position in the foundry industry with a ~71% market share (according to Q3 '25 Trendforce figures, the most up-to-date). No other fab is as dominant as TSMC and the second-strongest foundry play, Samsung, only represented a market share of 6.8%, followed by SMIC with a share of 5.1%.

Shares of TSMC are currently trading at a 19.9X P/E ratio, based off of FY 2027 expected earnings, which means they are still some of the cheapest in the industry group. Nvidia, AMD and ASML (ASML) are trading at forward P/E ratios of 24.1X, 21.8X and 32.6X and I have strong buy ratings on all of these companies given sustained growth tailwinds and soaring adoption of AI chips in U.S. Data Centers. Importantly, since TSMC reports its Q4 earnings way earlier than other chip players, the strong earnings scorecard bodes well Nvidia and AMD as well.

In my opinion, shares of TSMC could revalue to a ~25X price-to-earnings ratio, which would be more in-line with the industry group average of 24.5X. TSMC's strong market position, dominance in the fab sector and increasing reliance on the HPC segment in a rapidly growing total addressable market, suggests a strategic advantage that I feel is not yet fully priced into the company's valuation. A recognition of TSMC's potential could lead to a major revaluation of the firm's shares in 2026 as well as new all-time highs. A 25X forward P/E ratio implies a fair value for TSMC in the neighborhood of $412/share which calculates to 26% upside revaluation potential.

TSMC’s risk profile

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing is a leading global fab with operations in Southeast Asia as well as a growing manufacturing footprint in the United States. The company has gone on an aggressive expansion spree in the U.S. by boosting its chip manufacturing capabilities. Therefore, TSMC is most vulnerable to a rollback of AI spending plans on the part of hyperscalers. Further, supply and demand dynamics for semiconductors have been historically volatile so changes in demand could quickly trigger an adjustment in order volumes and lead to a drop-off in gross margins.

Closing thoughts

As TSMC’s revenue report earlier this month already indicated, the world’s leading fab is crushing it due to the ongoing AI splurge: TSMC is the ultimate beneficiary of this AI boom in the Data Center market, given its dominant foundry market position, and the chip enterprise should be able to remain highly profitable in 2026, especially with demand for AI-optimized chips set growing.

This year TSMC is likely to grow ~30% on a full-year basis, highlighting an attractive risk/reward relationship for investors since this growth seems underpriced to me, relative to other chip plays available. Maybe most importantly, TSMC is growing its gross profit margins and is becoming increasingly reliant on the high-margin HPC segment... which bodes well for earnings growth this year. TSMC is cheap with a P/E ratio below 20.0X and with shares undervalued by ~21%, I see a favorable investment setup for 2026 as well.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Brookfield: A Future $100 Stock Hiding In Plain Sight
data.description:
Brookfield Corporation (BN)(BN:CA) is a company that had my attention ever since I found out a while ago that Bill Ackman's Pershing Square Holdings (PSHZF) owns it, since I tend to like his stock picks. If you value Brookfield like a traditional finance company -- by using its P/E ratio or P/B ratio, for example -- the stock may look expensive to you. But when you look more under the hood, you realize this stock is actually underpriced.

Brookfield has many moving parts, such as its asset management business, its insurance business, and others. Brookfield will also benefit from growing AI infrastructure demand via its investments in AI data centers, power, and digital networks. It shouldn't be valued like a bank or traditional insurance company or just an asset manager. It's a complicated company that gave me a headache when trying to understand it.

Brookfield should be valued based on its distributable earnings, which have been growing nicely over the years and should continue growing at a 20%+ rate until 2030, according to management. We can also use management's sum-of-the-parts valuation, which values the company at $68 per share compared to its current price of $47. The company has also put its money where its mouth is, buying back shares because they're underpriced, although not a huge amount of shares, admittedly.

When you combine its impressive growth rate, sum of the parts valuation, and the modest forward distributable earnings multiple, the stock looks undervalued. Not only that, but this is a compounder with a strong track record of great performance (1,193% total return in the past 20 years). Therefore, I give BN stock a buy rating.

What Brookfield Corporation Actually Does

Brookfield Corporation fits into the asset management industry, but let's talk a bit more about what you get when you buy Brookfield.

First, according to BN's website, Brookfield has a 73% ownership interest in Brookfield Asset Management (BAM) (BAM:CA), which has over $1 trillion of assets under management and an $86 billion market cap. BAM earns fees for the money it manages.

Second, BN also owns a 26% interest in Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP), which owns and operates essential infrastructure and has a $16 billion market cap. Next, it owns a 46% interest in Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP), which has an $8.7 billion market cap; a 68% stake in Brookfield Business Partners (BBU), with a $3.1 billion market cap; and a 100% stake in Brookfield Property Group, as shown below.

Further, Brookfield owns and controls an insurance and retirement business called Brookfield Wealth Solutions (BNT). This company has a $14.7 billion market cap. BNT sells annuities and insurance policies, meaning it gets premiums upfront that won't have to be paid out for a while, giving BNT money to invest. Brookfield then invests that money into things it's familiar with, like renewables and infrastructure.

Here's what Bruce Flatt, the company's CEO, said specifically about Brookfield Wealth Solutions' strategy, during the Goldman Sachs 2025 U.S. Financial Services Conference:

So our strategy so far has been as opposed to running an insurance company which just invests into traditional -- in traditional ways into both liquid and private credit, our strategy has largely been a barbell approach, not unlike what Berkshire Hathaway has always done, which is hold very large amounts of cash, which we hold today. And that allows you to put significant money into equity strategies, which earn much higher returns.

So don't try to earn 7%, earn 3% in [ 14. ] And if you can earn 3% in [ 14, ] that averages to 7%. But in our view, you can, a, earn a higher return on average if you do your job right, but b, you can take less risk because of the things that we know that we're doing. And the advantage that we have is that the strategies that are the underpinning of Brookfield Asset Management are long-term yielding cash-flow-type assets in renewables, infrastructure, real estate that are very well treated and earn excellent low-risk returns in an insurance company.

Brookfield's various businesses give it access to lots of capital, and according to Bruce Flatt, "Having access to capital wins."

Brookfield's Distributable Earnings: The Figure That Matters

As stated in the intro, book value or EPS shouldn't be used to value Brookfield. You can use distributable earnings instead, which better represents the cash flow available to shareholders and isn't swayed by paper profits.

The company defines distributable earnings as "the sum of distributable earnings from our asset management business, distributable operating earnings from our wealth solutions business, distributions received from our ownership of investments, realized carried interest and disposition gains from principal investments, net of earnings from our Corporate Activities, preferred share dividends, and equity-based compensation costs."

There are also distributable earnings before realizations and capital allocation, which represent core earnings before selling assets at a profit or reinvesting capital into other businesses.

In the Q3-2025 earnings call, Brookfield mentioned that its TTM distributable earnings were $5.4 billion, or $2.27 per share, an 18% increase year-over-year. That's quite the impressive growth rate for an already-large company. That also translates to a distributable earnings multiple of 20.8x, telling a completely different story than the P/E ratio or even the price/book multiple.

If you check its GAAP P/E ratio, you'll see a figure of around 138x, which looks overvalued. Full stop. The price/book of 2.5x also looks pricey when you consider that the book value per share has been flat over the past few years while the stock has gone up.

That's why distributable earnings matter here. The sum of the parts valuation is important too, which I'll get to later.

The AI Opportunity

AI infrastructure is a big opportunity for Brookfield. As Bruce Flatt said during the 2025 Investor Day, it's a $7 trillion opportunity that can possibly be the largest business in the company within 10 years.

On AI, you've seen our slides. This is a $7 trillion opportunity. We're ideally suited because of our power, our adjacencies, our compute infrastructure, our real estate businesses to participate in this. We have a number of AI factories that we're working on, which is around a $200 billion investment project over the next while, and this is just the beginning, and it's really just the start of what's going on in the world on the infrastructure side. This is a multi-decade opportunity that possibly is the largest business within our company within 10 years.

Brookfield is building AI factories, and AI-related companies will pay Brookfield to use these factories. It doesn't matter which AI company wins. Brookfield will collect money either way. AI requires power and real estate, and luckily for Brookfield, it owns both.

The Stock Is Undervalued

At its 2025 Investor Day, Brookfield laid out a sum-of-the-parts valuation that pegs its fair value at $68, with the bulk of the value coming from asset management. At the current price of $47.29, the stock would have to increase by 43.8% to reach $68/share. It makes sense that as of Q3, BN bought back $950 million worth of its shares since management believes the company is undervalued. However, it's worth noting that even annualized, that's a buyback yield of just over 1%, which isn't that high.

Importantly, this plan value per share figure has been increasing. It grew at a 16% CAGR from 2020 to 2025, going from $32 to $68, exceeding management's expectations. Moreover, distributable earnings before realizations grew at an impressive 22% CAGR. Per the Investor Day presentation, the company actually expects a plan value of $140 per share by 2030, implying plenty of upside potential.

The valuation also looks interesting when considering management's forward estimates. For 2030, management has the following projections: $4.60 for DE per share before realizations and capital allocation (17% CAGR), and $6.95 per share for regular distributable earnings (25% CAGR).

That suggests a 10.3x forward multiple for 2030 if you use DE before realizations and capital allocation, or a 6.8x forward multiple for normal DE. Even if BN only ends up with a 15x multiple on its DE by 2030, we're talking about a $104.25 share price, or a 120% gain in five years. Of course, the future DE multiple can be higher than 15x, as it's currently over 20x.

It's not always a great thing to put too much trust in management projections, but given BN's strong track record, I don't see a major reason to doubt them.

Risks: What Could Go Wrong With My Thesis

What can go wrong with my thesis? First, companies like BN that own many other companies are often complicated, misunderstood, or hard to analyze. This can lead to a perpetual conglomerate discount, where the stock always trades at a discount to the sum of its parts because investors often prefer easier-to-analyze companies. So, the discount to the plan value per share is a good starting point, but it's not a guarantee that the gap between the share price and the plan value will close.

In addition, since the company is complex, some investors may not fully understand it, which can lead to panic selling in rough market conditions.

Another risk is relying on management's estimates for distributable earnings since they could be wrong. But as I stated above, I'm comfortable with this risk due to the company's track record.

Lastly, there's always the risk that future AI demand could be overestimated. Industry growth is truly hard to forecast. If data center growth slows, Brookfield's infrastructure investments could take longer to pay off.

The Bottom Line On BN Stock

BN stock is undervalued both when looking at a sum of its parts valuation and its forward distributable earnings multiple. I believe the stock can definitely be worth over $100/share in 5 years if management's distributable earnings estimates are met. Given the company's track record and the large AI opportunity at hand, I'm confident that can happen. What makes its track record more notable is that it was achieved across multiple market cycles and crises, cementing its status as a reliable long-term compounder worth owning.

data.title: Rexford Industrial Realty: A Growing 4% Yield For Lifelong Income
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

Whenever I travel, I frequently see new and old storage units all over.

A storage unit is essentially just a garage that's not attached to your home that you can throw items in that you don't need daily, and you refuse to get rid of. I can remember when I moved from one house to another, I got a storage unit that I thought I would have for just a couple of months. Three years later, I finally emptied that storage unit and stopped paying my rent for it. The owner of that storage facility commended me on closing that out and finalizing it because, as he put it, "basically no one does that." They pay the bill for the storage unit and forget about what's in there, never taking time to empty it.

Moving from the personal space to the commercial space, having facilities to store goods before they're sold and after they're manufactured is essential. Many companies love to produce only what they need, but this is not a reality that can persist. If you have an uptick in sales, you don't want to have a waitlist that can deter individuals from buying from your company again in the future. Amazon (AMZN) famously warehouses millions of products so they can deliver them with exceptional speed.

Having to own the buildings you keep your goods in can be an extremely high expense and a logistical nightmare for many companies. This is where real estate investment trusts, REITs, can step in and provide ownership of the buildings and rent them to companies.

Today, I want to look at a way to get paid and have a growing paycheck from a company that warehouses goods for others.

Let's dive in!

Get Paid For Storing Goods

Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR) acquires, manages, and redevelops prime infill industrial assets throughout Southern California. As of Sept. 30, 2025, the company owned roughly 51 million square feet of industrial real estate.

Without properties such as REXR's, the United States economy would be in a world of hurt. This is because an estimated 30% of total U.S. port volume is processed at the Ports of Los Angeles and Long Beach. By itself, Southern California is the planet's fourth-largest industrial market, only ranked behind the U.S., China, and Japan.

Combined with the constrained supply of industrial real estate in its markets, this affords the REIT a great deal of pricing power. REXR's average annual lease rate as of Q3 2025 was just above $17 per square foot. Through the first nine months of 2025, the industrial REIT has leased 7.4 million square feet with an average embedded rent step of 3.6%. That provides an organic means of growth for REXR.

The industrial REIT can also lean on redevelopment activity to drive growth in the years ahead. As of Q3 2025, CFO Michael Fitzmaurice pointed out in his opening remarks during the Q3 2025 Earnings Call that REXR had about $65 million of projected annualized NOI in redevelopments. Of that amount, $41 million was tied to projects that stabilized during the quarter or were in lease-up, with the remaining $24 million tied to properties under construction. This more than offsets the $25 million of annualized NOI in late 2025 and throughout 2026 that will be offline for redevelopment.

Overall, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is that REXR's core FFO per share will edge 3% higher in 2025 to $2.41. Another 2.1% increase in core FFO per share to $2.46 is anticipated for 2026. In 2027, an additional 4.9% jump in core FFO per share to $2.58 is the current consensus. For 2028, it's predicted that core FFO per share will rise by 4.7% to $2.70.

REXR is financially healthy as well. As of Sept. 30, 2025, its net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio was 4.1x. It also held $1.6 billion of liquidity.

REXR is a great income play as well, with its 4.4% dividend yield being about 4x greater than the S&P 500 index's (SP500) 1.1% yield. The industrial REIT's dividend is also secure, which is evidenced by a core FFO payout ratio that will likely be in the low-70% range for 2026. That's why I'm confident that REXR can build on its 12-year dividend growth streak. REXR has a 14.87% 5-year CAGR dividend growth rate. This generates an attractive 19.3% Chowder number.

REXR's shares look to be a decent value right now, too. From the current $39 share price (as of Jan. 1, 2026), the industrial REIT is trading at a forward 12-month P/FFO ratio of 15.7 (100% weight for 2026). This is substantially below the 10-year average P/FFO ratio of 28, according to FAST Graphs.

Moving forward, I believe that a fair value P/FFO ratio of 19 is rational. That's because this arguably factors in REXR's more modest growth prospects than in years past and the elevated interest rate environment. This implies shares are priced at a 17% discount to fair value.

If the industrial REIT matches growth forecasts and returns to fair value, it could post a 25% total return in 2026. By the end of 2030, REXR could generate 12% annual total returns.

Of course, the industrial REIT isn't without its risks. Its concentrated presence in Southern California brings key risks that should be monitored over time.

As Kody put it in his September article, California is fraught with natural disaster risk. In the most recent year of data (2023), over 7,000 wildfires destroyed almost 333,000 acres.

Aside from the wildfire risk, the other prominent natural disaster has to do with earthquakes: There are approximately 15,000 known faults in California, of which over 3% (500+) are classified as active. This almost guarantees a major earthquake happening in Southern California within the next 30 years, with a 93% probability of a 6.7 magnitude earthquake or stronger hitting the area. This could damage REXR's properties and disrupt operations for its tenants.

The company's properties are also somewhat indirectly dependent on steady trade with China. If relations are fractured and imports from other Asian trade partners can't pick up the slack, demand for REXR's properties could be pressured.

Conclusion

REXR is an extremely attractive REIT that stores goods for others. They provide an essential piece of the supply chain puzzle. As companies in the United States continue to focus on onshoring manufacturing to avoid tariffs, a place to store goods within the United States becomes more and more essential.

My continual goal as a dividend investor is to collect a steady dividend paycheck from every aspect of life around me. I do this by owning extremely high-quality companies that pay a steady and growing dividend. I'm also looking for companies that are undervalued. This allows my portfolio to not only see a steadily growing dividend stream, but also exceptionally strong total returns. My portfolio becomes a fortress on a hill, allowing me to live in splendor and quality because of the companies I hold and the dividends they provide.

You can't be a king without a castle. You can't be a dividend king if you're not collecting dividends. Come be royalty with me and be a Dividend King.

data.title: Altius Minerals: A Truly Overlooked Opportunity At This Altitude Of Share Price (Upgrade)
data.description:
Market Sentiment Is Clear: Optimism – And We Share This View

Altius Minerals Corporation (ATUSF) is benefiting from a very positive market sentiment, which we continue to share and with which we now feel even more aligned. The outlook reinforces our confidence in the future, even after a total return of +62.59% (share price +61.62% plus dividend yield +0.97%) since our last analysis on July 11, 2025, which has outperformed the S&P 500's performance of +10.21%.

An Update Is Needed

There are profitability factors—such as the potash and copper markets—that appear particularly promising given their central role in agriculture and clean energy, and are crucial to Altius' success within its corporate structure. While the market eagerly awaits the impact of these factors, here is our update on their critical importance to Altius and something else.

Our Previous Coverage

In previous analyses, we rated Altius Minerals Corporation shareholders favorably, as they benefited from dividend payments driven by the favorable commodity price environment. Furthermore, the stock's long-term growth potential appeared promising. The stock's volatility reflects fluctuations in the commodity market. We therefore also considered the possibility of price declines, which would offer investors an opportunity to strengthen their positions. The company's solid fundamentals and the royalties from the sale of essential metals in the electrification and sustainability sectors offer positive long-term prospects. Nevertheless, we liked participants prioritising the lower market value to offset the stock's low trading volume and volatility.

About Altius Minerals Corporation

If you are an investor or interested in investing in Altius Minerals Corporation, you should be aware that you are bound to the company's mission (see the description under “About Altius” in the Q3 2025 results) to increase the share price over the long term. In our view, the company offers an investment product for long-term investors. This share price growth is primarily driven by earnings. As a precursor of growth and dividend payouts, earnings are an event that attracts the market. If this is repeated over an extended period, the market develops a positive attitude toward the stock and invests accordingly, leading to a revaluation of the share price. This is a proven principle in the US stock market and also applies to Altius. This dynamic now needs to become a consistent feature to ensure that the long-term investors that Altius apparently wants to attract remain loyal to the stock. The partnership between Altius and Market is not only crisis-proof but also pays off in the long run: ATUSF achieved a return of +66.97% over the last 12 months, +179.46% over the past five years, and +470.80% over the past ten years.

For comparison, SPX S&P 500 futures achieved +66.97% over the last 12 months, +82.81% over the past five years, and +270.39% over the past ten years (as of today). Since SPX S&P 500 futures are considered the benchmark for the entire US stock market, Altius is exceptionally well-positioned. Backed by long-term contracts, Altius generates royalties from trading commodities that are essential to the economy. It may be hard to believe, but these commodities are at the heart of a global project in which Western economic powers and their Eastern rivals, most notably China and India, are competing on various fronts. The driving forces behind this competition are challenges such as sustainability, the energy transition from fossil fuels to clean energy sources, and the reduction of environmental impact through the use of renewable energies.

Added to this is the electrification of the transportation sector with the gradual introduction of electric vehicles and their lithium and cobalt batteries. Replacing coal-fired furnaces with electric furnaces in heavy industry and agriculture also contributes to higher crop yields and helps avert a looming food crisis. The raw materials in which Altius invests include copper, electricity from renewable energy sources, key battery metals such as lithium, nickel, and cobalt, clean iron ore, and potash.

In a separate portfolio segment under the heading "Project Generation," Altius initiates mining projects that can be monetized through sale to interested mining companies. Altius could also acquire stakes in project developers or receive royalties, which would strengthen its core business.

It is therefore not surprising that Altius is listed in the S&P/TSX Small Cap, the S&P/TSX Global Mining, and the S&P/TSX Canadian Dividend Aristocrats indices.

Altius Minerals As of Q3 2025

For clarification: All amounts are stated in thousands of Canadian dollars, except for amounts per share, unless otherwise noted.

Altius Minerals reports on a solid third quarter of 2025:

  • Revenue was $17.2 million, an increase of 32.3% compared to $13 million in the prior year.
  • Attributable royalties amounted to $21.4 million ($0.46 per share), an increase of 45.9% compared to $14.7 million in the prior year.

Growth Drivers:

  • Higher potash volumes.
  • Higher copper shipments.
  • Higher royalties from power generation.
  • Significantly increased interest and investment income.

Declines:

  • Lower iron ore dividends.

Increased royalty revenues contributed significantly to the growth in key earnings figures:

  • Adjusted EBITDA of $13.2 million, an increase of 40.4% year-over-year.
  • Adjusted operating cash flow of $15.4 million, an increase of 51% year-over-year.
  • Adjusted net income of $7.7 million, an increase of 203.3% year-over-year.

  • Net income rose to $264.9 million, an 83-fold increase compared to the previous year. This was due to a substantial one-time gain, as Altius sold a gold royalty interest very profitably, the Arthur Gold.

Strengthening the Financial Situation

Improved profitability metrics contributed to a strengthened financial position. As the screenshots show, the free cash flow generated after debt reduction was used for dividend payments to shareholders. These figures underscore Altius's strong financial position, characterized by significant debt reduction and the transition to positive net cash position. Sequential growth rates.

Shareholder Value Creation

Altius was thus able to strengthen its focus on shareholder returns through dividends and share buybacks while managing debt reduction. $10.8 million in free cash flow in the September quarter of 2025, which was fully used as follows: $3 million for dividend payments, $1.1 million for share buybacks, and $7.9 million for debt reduction.

Altius used some of its liquidity to manage the cash flow financing, but the slight cash drawdown was barely noticeable, as it was overshadowed by the substantial proceeds from the Arthur Gold sale. Actually, Altius also received $81.4 million from Triple Flag's acquisition of Orogen's 1.0% NSR royalty on Arthur, representing a $64 million realised gain.

The sale dramatically reinforced the long-term growth prospects for Altius' core portfolio and shareholder value for many years to come, well beyond the third quarter of 2025. For most companies, this sale would have been exceptional, but for Altius, it was part of its core business.

Growth Projects

Altius' operational royalty portfolio generates revenue from copper, potash, nickel, cobalt, iron ore, and lithium—cornerstones of the economy—and has an average life of over 4 decades. These commodities are influenced by secular macro trends such as the energy transition, electrification, the use of AI, and the increasing demand for potash in agriculture due to global food security and sustainable farming practices. These macro-trends can occasionally lead to sharp price fluctuations. The war economy can cause short-term price spikes.

The project currently under development could secure Altius another 15 to 20 years of revenues from key commodities in its core portfolio, with copper and iron ore dominating, but precious metals and zinc, an essential metal for infrastructure and construction, also playing a role. Altius sees a positive development for its core portfolio regarding the future of its 3% gross sales royalty interest in the Kami iron ore open-pit mining project in Canada, and the future of its 2% NSR royalty in the El Domo Curipamba project in Ecuador. Based on their valuations—US$500 million for the Kami Iron Mine Partnership between Champion Iron Limited, Nippon Steel Corporation, and Sojitz Corporation, and US$240.5 million in initial capital costs for the development of the Curipamba project by Silvercorp Metals Inc.—these assets are expected to generate high royalty revenues for Altius in the future. The royalty rates for Kami and Curipamba fall within the usual range of Altius' existing royalty revenues.

It should be noted, however, that these projects are still in the development phase compared to Altius's already operational portfolio of royalties. Production at Kami is expected to commence well after 2026, as several years may pass after the completion of the feasibility study, expected by the end of 2026. Production at Curipamba is planned to begin by the end of 2026.

Price Outlook For The Most Important Commodities

  • Copper: Seeking Alpha recently reported on research from the firm "consulting S&P Global", which shows that the rise of artificial intelligence and defence could lift copper demand by 50% by 2040, and that supply will not be able to keep pace. Given this fundamental upward trend, copper futures (HG1:COM) are expected to rise further. At the time of writing, they stood at $13,382.11 per tonne, up 4.5% from $12,800 when Seeking Alpha reported on the S&P Global research report.
  • According to this article on Investing.com from Wednesday, January 14, 2026, Morgan Stanley expects rising potash prices due to strong global demand, which is having an excellent positive impact on Nutrien's financials. Mosaic, on the other hand, faces some challenges in its phosphate business, but these headwinds appear minor and only temporary, which is why the analyst maintained its “Equalweight” rating. Except for potash royalty from the Canadian Esterhazy mine operated by The Mosaic Company, the other Canadian mines of Rocanville, Cory, Allan, Patience Lake, and Vanscoy are operated by Nutrien Ltd.
  • According to the website of Altius, as the principal shareholder of Labrador Iron Ore Royalty Corporation (“LIORC”), Altius receives distributions from LIORC, which in turn holds a 15.10% stake in the Iron Ore Company of Canada (“IOC”). Altius states:

IOC is a producer and exporter of premium iron ore pellets and high-grade concentrate with average iron grades in excess of 66%. The iron produced at the mine commands a premium for its high grade/low impurity concentrates and pellets that require less metallurgical coal usage in steel-making plants.

Altius’ distributions received by LIORC are financed as follows. Therefore, Altius also states:

LIORC [...] receives a 7% gross overriding royalty and a 10 cent per tonne commission on all iron ore products produced, sold and shipped by IOC.

In light of the distributions Altius receives from LIORC's 15.10% stake in IOC, Trading Economics’ latest report on the iron ore market can be interpreted optimistically. Of all the factors that enhance the profitability of iron ore companies and thus the dividend potential for Altius, the Trading Economics report highlights key drivers such as record imports from China, low inventories, improved steel margins, supply bottlenecks from Australia and Brazil, and the anticipated package of measures to stimulate domestic demand in PRC. Therefore, Trading Economics writes at this time:

Iron ore futures climbed to around CNY 825 per ton, reaching their strongest level since February last year after China reported record imports of the steelmaking ingredient for both December and full year 2025. The surge was driven by low inventories, improved steel margins, and restocking ahead of the Lunar New Year holiday in February. China also posted record steel exports last month as traders front loaded shipments ahead of Beijing’s planned export license requirement for 2026. Supply side conditions added further support, with industry data pointing to slower shipments from Australia and Brazil in the latest week. Sentiment was further underpinned by expectations of additional policy easing after China’s cabinet met on Friday to discuss a package of fiscal and financial measures aimed at boosting domestic demand, including steps to support household consumption.

  • The high demand for electric vehicles is a major reason for the structural deficit in the lithium market, which is why prices are expected to rise going forward. This is according to statistics from the “Lithium Market Outlook” by Canaccord Genuity Equity Research dated November 19, 2025.

A further boost: In its Q3 2025 report, Altius states:

Following a period of increased regulatory uncertainty, ARR/GBR have noted an increased level of market activity both in terms of the acquisition of development stage projects from its various royalty based investee companies and the demand for its royalty capital as part of construction and operating stage project finance initiatives. These factors are providing the potential for enhanced royalty portfolio growth over the coming quarters.

Altius Minerals Corporation on the US OTCQX

Altius Minerals Corporation’s U.S. OTCQX-listed shares under the symbol “ATUSF” were trading at $32.36/share, giving it a market capitalisation of $1.50 billion, as of this writing.

Altius Minerals Corporation paid a quarterly dividend of $0.10 per share on December 15, 2025, to shareholders and under the Dividend Reinvestment Plan (DRIP) in common shares to residents in Canada and countries offshore the United States. The dividend results in a dividend yield (“FWD”) of 0.89% compared to the sector median of 1.70% at the time of writing.

Technical Analysis

The current share price is 34.7% above its 52-week average of $24.015 (range of $15.14 to $32.89). The following structure—the 20-day moving average (20-MA) above the 50-day moving average (50-MA), which in turn is above the 200-day moving average (200-MA)—suggests that shares of Altius Minerals Corporation, listed on the US OTCQX, are in a sustained uptrend in both the short and long term. Because Altius Minerals Corporation is sensitive to fluctuations in commodity prices during the overall uptrend, it is possible that if any of these commodities, particularly copper and potash or iron ore, experience a sharp, temporary downturn, Altius shares could fall back to levels that offer a better buying opportunity than the current levels. These levels are not far from overbought levels in the current cycle, as the RSI of 71.30 reliably demonstrates. However, there is no guarantee that cyclical fluctuations will actually lead to the lower value a market participant may be looking for. This entails a risk.

Valuation

These comparisons between Altius and the sector median against the stated financial indicators support a buy signal.

  • EV/Sales (FWD) of 28.44 versus sector median of 1.95.
  • P/E GAAP (TTM) of 5.89 versus sector median of 24.90.
  • Price/Book (TTM) of 2.45 versus sector median of 2.52.

A comparison of the price-to-earnings ratio (P/E ratio)—which, while favourable for Altius, is nonetheless unusual and stands in stark contrast to the other two indicators that would suggest overvaluation – shows that Altius benefited from the sale of Arthur Gold for approximately $287.7 million, thereby boosting earnings. This likely fueled significant demand for Altius shares. In addition to the effect on GAAP earnings, which increased sharply, the stock's low liquidity further amplified the upward pressure on the share price.

Therefore, the market valuation appears overvalued relative to adjusted earnings, leading most market participants to the obvious conclusion: wait and see. Future price fluctuations might offer a more favourable entry point. In our view, this approach carries considerable risk, as the strong growth prospects for copper, potash, and other commodities are doing everything they can to dash any hopes of a substantial price decline. These market participants may be overlooking important opportunities for Altius, particularly those arising from the third quarter of 2025 onward.

For example, the $353.3 million in cash flow generated from the sale of Arthur Gold royalties can also be considered part of Altius' core business. If one does not view this as a one-off event, but rather as an event that, while not frequent, can nevertheless occur in the core business, then the company is incredibly undervalued compared to its industry peers after this sale. The sale will generate substantial cash flow for Altius, potentially even exceeding that of strategically more important royalty assets in the portfolio, assuming they are divested. These assets include copper, potash, iron ore, lithium, nickel, and cobalt.

Although gold is not one of the most important commodities in Altius's portfolio, the company achieves exceptionally high returns from it, thanks to its historical upward price momentum. Without foregoing future strategic royalty revenues for its portfolio, which includes key commodities linked to the aforementioned secular trends (electrification, energy transition, AI, electric vehicles, etc.), Altius is benefiting from the favourable momentum of record gold prices. Long before Altius reaches a comparable cash level through years of profitability, it suddenly has substantial cash reserves. It can now use these to further develop and expand its long-term core business and face future volatile market conditions with significantly less apprehension. Altius emerges stronger from the competition, and these aspects, overlooked by many market participants, make the stock very attractive, even if this is not immediately apparent from adjusted earnings or other comparative metrics.

The Risk

  • The greatest risk lies in the volatility of commodities. As previously explained, the key commodities in Altius' portfolio—apart from short-term fluctuations—benefit from long-term trends, which is why we assess the risk in this area as very low. The economy is being constructed on the energy transition, electrification, electromobility, and artificial intelligence.
  • The low trading volume on the US stock exchange (average volume over the last three months: 13,590 shares as of this writing) has its drawbacks. For example, it can lead to selling prices falling below the average buying price if investors are forced to sell shares because the market cannot execute their sell orders as they would like. On the other hand, a sudden and intense flood of demand for Altius shares also offers the potential for a rapid price increase.
  • Country risk: The portfolio is heavily focused on North American and stable economies.

Conclusion

Altius Minerals Corporation is benefiting from a very positive market sentiment, which is entirely justified. The stock now presents an attractive opportunity, with its solid portfolio of royalty revenues and exceptionally strong financial position. Altius Minerals has more financial flexibility to capitalise on the promising prospects for key commodities such as copper, potash, iron ore, and the metal crucial for electrification and electromobility. Its strategy for generating shareholder returns is currently very well positioned. The risk is that not buying now could prove to be a regrettable decision once the market starts to appropriately value the substantial capital generated by the sale of the Arthur Gold royalty.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
